data.title: Nvidia, Dell, Salesforce To Report Earnings Next Week
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major averages on Friday closed out a topsy-turvy week with losses, as concerns over fiscal debt and U.S. President Donald Trump's latest salvo of tariff threats weighed on sentiment. The focus of the week was the U.S. House's passing of Trump's controversial tax bill by the narrow vote of 215-214. According to the nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office, the proposed tax changes would increase the federal deficit by an estimated $3.8T over the next decade. It also noted that the tax cuts would benefit top earners more than low-income households. The bill could lead to around 7.6M Americans losing their health insurance over the next 10 years, the agency estimated.

As Trump's tax bill heads to the Senate, where it is expected to meet sharp resistance, investors will keep a close eye on the economic events of next week, which will impact future market sentiment. Of note, Monday is a stock market holiday due to Memorial Day.

Important economic indicators due to be out next week include Durable Goods Orders, House Price Index, and Consumer Confidence on Tuesday, while Wednesday will include Richmond Manufacturing Index and FOMC Meeting Minutes. Traders should keep an eye out for reports on Corporate Profits, GDP Price Index, and Core PCE Prices, due to be published on Thursday, with Personal Income, Personal Spending, Retail Inventories, and Wholesale inventories due on Friday.

Nvidia (NVDA), Salesforce (CRM), HP (HPQ), Dell (DELL), ELF Beauty (ELF), Ulta Beauty (ULTA), and Costco (COST) will report earnings next week.

______________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, May 26: JOYY (JOYY). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, May 27: Okta (OKTA). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, May 28: Nvidia (NVDA), Salesforce (CRM), HP (HPQ), ELF Beauty (ELF). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, May 29: Costco (COST), Dell (DELL), Marvell (MRVL), Li Auto (LI), Ulta Beauty (ULTA), Best Buy (BBY). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, May 30: Canopy Growth (CGC). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Navitas Semiconductor (NVTS) and UroGen Pharma (URGN) have a higher level of implied volatility than normal based on options trading. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include NervGen Pharma (OTCQB:NGENF), SatixFy Communications (SATX), and Regulus Therapeutics (RGLS). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Spirit Aviation (FLYY), Compass Diversified (CODI), and B&G Foods (BGS).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Skyworks Solutions (SWKS), Warner Music Group (WMG), Electronic Arts (EA), Expedia (EXPE), CSX (CSX), eBay (EBAY), Kraft Heinz (KHC), NetEase (NTES), and T-Mobile US (TMUS). Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

Investor Events: The three-day Bitcoin 2025 Conference will run from May 27-29 and feature talks by Vice President JD Vance, Strategy (MSTR) CEO Michael Saylor, and Robinhood Markets (HOOD) CEO Vlad Tenev. On Wednesday, the 60th session of the Joint Ministerial Monitoring Committee of OPEC and non-OPEC countries is scheduled to take place in Vienna, Austria. And finally, on Friday, the five-day ASCO Conference will begin and will include several oncology presentations.

Nvidia Earnings Preview: AI-behemoth Nvidia is set to report its first quarter earnings next week. A consensus of analysts expect the company to earn $0.73 per share on $43.18B for the coming quarter. Investment firm Oppenheimer recently expressed some caution going into the print as analyst Rick Schafer pointed out that the company is likely to top estimates for the first-quarter, but its guidance will likely be "roughly in-line," despite the loss of the H20 chip in China.

Dell Earnings Preview: Dell will report its first quarter earnings next week, with analysts seeing earnings per share of $1.69 on a revenue of $23.17B. Morgan Stanley expects a slight April quarter beat, but unchanged fiscal year guidance as Dell balances AI infrastructure momentum with mixed traditional infrastructure checks and volatile tariff backdrop. The analysts added that given limited near-term estimates upside and recent outperformance, the stock may take a breather at earnings, and noted that they still like the second half of the year setup.

data.title: A Great Income Pick For Your Retirement: EPR Properties
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

We live in an era that's marked by a desire to have things immediately. It's extremely normal to expect two-day shipping on Amazon (AMZN) Prime, or sometimes even same-day delivery. Walmart (WMT) delivery can show up at your door within a few hours. We love the convenience of getting things right away and not having to plan for the future. This has leaked into many different aspects of our society's thinking. We're very impatient. We want things done immediately and to see those results immediately. Rarely do we like to play on the side of delayed gratification.

When this bleeds into the investing realm, it causes many investors to never actually become true investors. Instead, they treat the stock market like a casino – walking up to a slot machine, putting in a coin, and pulling the arm. They buy a security at one price and hope that the slot machine of the market will boost it quickly so that it can sell out within a matter of minutes, hours, or even days. They then try to repeat this process. It's a big reason that online day trading courses or advertisements are very popular – people want to win big and win quickly! The big difference between the successes of an investor in the market who holds for the long term and picks carefully versus the rapid trader can be summed up in a single quote:

"The stock market is a device for transferring money from the impatient to the patient" – Warren Buffett

Today, I want to look at a company whose management team carries out careful, deliberate actions. The company has performed wonderfully historically and over its entire lifespan has readily bested the market in total returns, but the average trader would avoid them because they're not willing to be patient and enjoy the rewards that come from that.

Let's dive in

Tortoise and the Hare

EPR Properties (NYSE:EPR), yielding 6.8%, is a REIT that owns "experiential properties". These are properties where people go to do fun things. Originally, a REIT that focused on movie theaters with AMC (AMC) as its sole tenant, EPR has diversified over the years to own waterparks, ski resorts, amusement parks, museums, destination hotels, Top Golf centers, and more. Most recently, EPR acquired its first regular golf property, acquiring the land and providing a mortgage loan for the golf club.

EPR has been on an excellent run this year, soundly besting the market (SP500)(SPY):

This has been heavily the result of management's highly methodical approach, as we've covered previously. The question we must ask, with the recent earnings release, is whether this approach continues to show fruit.

EPR wants to reduce its theater exposure as well as its exposure to education properties. However, EPR isn't dumping properties cheaply just to adjust its portfolio. Instead, EPR has taken its time and is selling for profits. In Q1, EPR sold three theaters and 11 education properties for $78.9 million, resulting in a gain on the sale of $9.4 million.

In Q1, EPR deployed $37.6 million across various property types. Source

EPR is looking to improve its diversification, but it's doing so while making a profit. With EPR's focus on doing things well, they saw strong growth in Q1 across all the important metrics: Source

These metrics improved even as their debt/EBITDA declined from 5.5x to 5.3x. Despite raising their dividend in Q1, their AFFO payout ratio is still only 71%.

As a result of their early success, EPR increased guidance for 2025:

EPR keeps moving forward, posting better numbers and growing its portfolio in a methodical manner. Management recognizes that the cost of capital isn't the best right now and isn't willing to issue equity to grow for the sake of growing. Instead, they're keeping their eyes on what's best for shareholders in the long run – using retained cash and capital recycling to produce steady growth.

This slow and steady approach might be frustrating for impatient investors who want everything right now. We must remember the tortoise won the race. EPR is moving forward. Every quarter is a little bit better than the prior one. We're happy to sit back and collect our dividend while we watch the grass grow at EPR's newest golf course.

Conclusion

Over its entire lifespan, EPR has performed wonderfully. Look over its entire life span, including the massive calamity that hit its total returns in 2020. EPR has readily topped the market (SPY)(SP500), almost doubling its return in the exact same amount of time:

Is EPR a perfect company? Definitely not. Management has goals to reduce exposure to some sectors and grow it to others based on current market conditions. They're not willing to make rapid changes just for the sake of rapid changes. They are careful and deliberate. This allows them to be able to sell properties for a profit and reward shareholders effectively. At the end of the day, a management team that cares more about carefully rewarding shareholders is worth holding because it produces long-term success.

In your retirement, the last thing most of you want is a company that's trying to dazzle and shock the market with rapid short-term changes. Likewise, you don't want to have the stress of having to constantly trade each day to unlock the money you need to live. You can take a slow and deliberate approach, collect wonderful income from the market, and never have to sell a share you don't want to sell. Those dividends are freshly minted dollars to your account that can never be taken away. Unlike a share price value that can move day to day, this money is cash in hand, and that cash in your hand is filled with possibilities for you to explore and discover.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: TreeHouse Foods: Efficiency Gains Priced In, Fair Valuation Supports Hold
data.description:
TreeHouse Foods: Fairly Valued With Margin Upside and Leverage Risk

TreeHouse Foods (NYSE:THS) may not be ripe for picking just yet, but it’s worth keeping on the shelf. TreeHouse is trading at a forward P/E and EV/EBITDA close compared to its peers, with balanced potential upsides and risks, which made me assign a hold recommendation.

At the moment, THS has shown a decline in revenue and EPS quarter-over-quarter. In addition, It carries high debt of $1.58 B to just $16 MM in cash with a high debt-to-EBITDA ratio at 4.97x above the industry benchmark. On the other hand, THS is expected to have margin expansion driven by its profitability improvement plan, and macro tailwinds presented in private-label industry growth at a CAGR of 11.7% by 2030. THS is well-positioned to capture more of the market, also possible strategic acquisitions in high-margin segments such as its purchase of Harris Tea earlier this year.

Quiet Leader in Private-Label, Focused on Margin and Scale

TreeHouse Foods, Inc. is a leading private-label food and beverage manufacturer, providing a variety of packaged goods like snacking products such as crackers, pretzels, in addition to beverage and drink mixes, including non-dairy creamer, coffee, broths and other blends.

THS supplies its variety of products to big retail grocery chains like Walmart and Kroger, food service distributors and other customers.

TreeHouse operates 27 manufacturing facilities across the U.S. and Canada. The company is currently focusing on margin expansion, cost savings, and category leadership in private label food. It continues to reshape its portfolio through acquisitions such as Harris Tea and pickle brands from Smucker's and exits like ready-to-drink beverages to concentrate on higher-margin, high-growth categories.

As per 10-Q, THS is focusing on acquiring capabilities rather than simply expanding its portfolio. In order to dominate high-growth, high-margin niches in private-label foods, evident by its recent acquisition that came on 2nd January of Harris Tea, the leading private-label tea manufacturer.

Stable Operations Amid Cost Pressures and Modest Outlook

THS has witnessed several headwinds in Q1 2025, reflected in a drop in dollar sales by 3.5%. This was driven by the recall of the frozen griddle products and a slight decrease in sales volume, and unfavorable product mix. Despite its exit from the ready to drink RTD beverage business, that created a short-term revenue headwind. The management viewed it as a strategic move necessary for THS to align with its long-term growth in order to invest in high-margin segments, as per 10-Q.

However, the dip was partially offset by Harris Tea sales that contributed 5% of total revenue in the first quarter, according to the management.

The management guidance for revenue this year is expected to increase or decrease by about 1%, which is understandable for a mature business like TreeHouse Foods.

The growth in revenue for the rest of the year will be supported by the resumption of the frozen griddle plant that is expected to be fully operating by the second half of 2025.

THS’ gross profit has seen an improvement as it increased by 2.8% quarter-over-quarter, despite the decline in the top line. The improvement in gross margin came primarily due to the execution of supply chain savings initiatives, as TreeHouse’s management initiated a target in 2024 of achieving $250 MM in supply chain savings. As presented in the slide, THS’ plan for minimizing its costs to reach $250 MM in savings by 2027.

In my view, the main driver for THS earnings growth depends on managing its costs rather than revenue growth. My view is based on its established market position as it already supplies giants like Walmart, Kroger, Costco, Amazon, Target and Aldi with private-label products in various categories; hence, expanding its market share is relatively limited unless it acquires high-growth segments.

Tariffs and an economic slowdown pushing toward recession will come in favor of private label manufacturers like TreeHouse, as consumers are looking for cheaper options. This will prompt big retailers like Walmart and Kroger to expand their private label sourcing, according to Morning Star. However, in this case, THS could face margin pressure as retailers may demand for lower prices. Still, TreeHouse can manage these challenges through its diversified revenue streams, large-scale operations and ongoing cost-saving initiatives.

On the other hand, the management has shown commitment toward savings as evidenced by its EBITDA growth by a solid 27.3% QoQ. They have guided toward achieving between $345 to $375 MM by the end of 2025, that will represent around 17% YoY growth. The table below summarizes key Q1 2025 financial metrics, highlighting improvements in gross profit and EBITDA.

Earnings Under Pressure, Rebound on the Horizon

TreeHouse saw a drop in EPS in 2024, largely due to the Listeria contamination that resulted in a recall of frozen griddle products by THS, and the restoration of one of its broth facilities. In general, THS witnessed a YoY decline in both top and bottom line in 2024, where its revenue declined by 2.3%.

In Q1 '25, THS witnessed a sharp decline in its GAAP earnings per share, reporting a net loss of $0.63 per share, that came primarily due to a non-recurring, non-cash hedging loss of around $24 MM as per their 10-Q. The normalized EPS that eliminates non-recurring and non-cash items accounted for $0.03, beating SA estimates by $0.19.

While the estimated EPS for FY 2025 represents a YoY drop by around 5%, the EPS is estimated to rebound in 2026 and 2027 driven by the full implementation of the profitability improvement plan, increasing sales volume by recent acquisitions like Harris Tea. In addition, the private-label food industry is expected to grow at a CAGR of 11.7%, reaching $283.36 B by 2030, according to Research and Markets. The growth is driven by a consumer shift toward plant-based and premium private-label products, which aligns with THS’ diversified product portfolio.

TreeHouse Foods: Not Overpriced, Not Risk-Free

I have compared TreeHouse Foods to six similar companies, as presented in the table below. The relative valuation using P/E and EV/EBITDA multiples have shown that TreeHouse’s valuation sits near peer averages, with slight premiums reflecting expected margins improvement. THS is exposed to risks associated with a high debt-to-EBITDA ratio [TTM] at around 4.9x which is way above the industry healthy range at 2.6x. The company’s total debt stands at $1.58 B while its cash balance accounts only for $16.4 million, which is a clear financial risk. It uses the debt to supply its acquisitions in order to grow, however this high amount of debt makes it exposed to any rising interest rates and limits its financial flexibility. The debt could be offset by any further divestitures.

On a P/E basis, TreeHouse is trading at a modest premium above peer median (11.35x), which shows that the market is pricing TreeHouse’s cost-saving potential, and the overall growth in the private-label food market.

From an EV-EBITDA perspective, TreeHouse Forward EV/EBITDA at 7.66x is lower than its Trailing Twelve Months ratio at 8.5x. This reflects the expected growth in EBITDA, which aligns with the management’s guidance. THS trading at 7.66x cheaper than the peers’ median 8.14x, while trading at almost equal to the peers’ average 7.6x.

Altogether, TreeHouse Foods is fairly priced relative to the peers, which implies a hold recommendation.

Despite its potential upsides outlined in its profitability improvement plan and surging EBITDA growth, while THS is exposed to risks associated with high debt, revenue declined consecutively in 2023, 2024 and Q1 2025. In addition to instability in earnings and limited revenue growth.

Key Takeaway

TreeHouse is trading at a fair valuation relative to peers, and with its balanced catalysts and risks, makes it a hold, not a buy, at least until the results of the second quarter are released. If gross and EBITDA margins witness improvement, and earnings grow, it can become a buy. However, considering the financial risk and its timid revenue, it is not attractive to buy.

data.title: Fidelis Insurance: Attractive Valuation As One-Time Pressures Pass
data.description:
Shares of Fidelis Insurance Holdings (NYSE:FIHL) have been a disappointing performer over the past year, losing about 2% even as the insurance sector has performed very well. This underperformance has left shares at a very low valuation relative to peers. The company suffered significant losses related to the Russia-Ukraine war as well as the California wildfire, which has weighed on results. To the extent these events are one-time in nature, I believe there is a significant opportunity in shares.

Fidelis is a diversified, specialty insurer with a solid track record

Fidelis is a specialty property and casualty insurer and reinsurer. As you can see below, it has broad exposure across a variety of niche asset classes, like marine, aviation, and credit finance. In addition to this, like most Bermuda-domiciled insurers, it has a substantial reinsurance portfolio, accounting for about one-fifth of the business. Through this reinsurance business, Fidelis does have meaningful exposure to catastrophe events like the Atlantic Hurricane season and disasters like the California wildfires. As a result, reinsurance results can be very volatile.

Bermuda’s loose regulatory and favorable tax regimes have made it a central hub for the global insurance industry. While the regulatory environment is favorable, I would note that the tax regime is not as favorable as it once was. There is now a 15% Bermuda tax, as the island seeks to maintain compliance with global minimum corporate tax agreements. I do not expect further increases in the corporate tax, and Fidelis has $90 million of deferred tax assets which it can use before paying the tax. In my valuation analysis, I assume an effective tax rate of 15% going forward.

As you can see below, Fidelis has historically had a combined ratio of 87-90%. As a reminder, a combined ratio of 100% means an insurer breaks even on its underwriting of policies. At 90%, they make a 10% margin. In the specialty space, I view maintaining a sub-90% ratio as critical to validating the quality of underwriting. While Fidelis has historically done this, in 2024, underwriting barely broke even, and in 2025’s first quarter, there was a loss. The question is whether this is a one-time degradation or a sign of things to come.

Russia's pressures have been significant but are unlikely to recur

I believe the answer here is nuanced. Its 2024 results were weighed down by the Russia-Ukraine war. As a result of the conflict and sanctions, dozens of planes owned by aircraft lessors like AerCap (AER) were stranded in Russia immediately with no means to recover them. As a result, they sought insurance recoveries on the losses, and Fidelis had exposure here through its aviation line. FIHL took $287 million of reserves in Q4 for aviation losses. These losses all relate to policies from before 2022.

On the most recent earnings call, Fidelis said it has settled ~80% of its exposure. There is one large English trial, which has up to $150 million of potential risk. After that, the issue will be 95% complete. I do expect some adverse outcome here, though perhaps not up to the $150 million. I am assuming for now a $1 per share, or $111 million after-tax impact, as the aircraft lessors have had a strong legal case.

Ultimately, I view these losses as one-time in nature. Russia-Ukraine has been the largest ground war involving a major power, arguably, since Vietnam. On top of this, Russian carriers relied on significant amounts of leases for their planes, creating a perfect storm of losses for the industry. Given the sanctions, Russia felt no need to honor the payments, and it was not really possible to physically recover the planes without invading Russia. I do not see losses here as repeatable or as a sign of poor underwriting. It was a genuine “Black Swan” event.

California hit Q1 results, but some level of catastrophes will occur annually

Next, looking at its most recent results, the California wildfires had a clear negative impact. In the company’s first quarter, Fidelis lost $0.38, which was actually a penny ahead of estimates. Gross written premiums grew by 14% to $1.72 billion. Fidelis had a 115.6% combined ratio, up from 85.8% last year as there were $333 million of catastrophe losses.

Overall, Fidelis took $167 million of California wildfire losses after taxes and reinstatement premiums that totaled $80 million. This led to a $95 million Q1 underwriting loss. These losses largely resided in its reinsurance business, Because of reinstatement premiums when insurers have to “re-fill” policies after all reinsurance coverage is used up, reinsurance premiums doubled to $91 million. Its combined ratio was 184%, leading to a $76 million loss.

Reinsurance underwriting margins are extremely volatile, depending on the magnitude of catastrophes. As you can see, in 2022, when there was a bad hurricane season, FIHL broke even. In 2023 and 2024, it generated massive profits during quieter hurricane seasons. 2025 has started off badly, given the magnitude of the fires. Ultimately, the nature of the hurricane season will determine whether FIHL generates a reinsurance profit for the full year or not.

Catastrophe events are uncorrelated and random to a degree—California fires have no impact on the probability of a bad hurricane season, for instance. Naturally, some years will be better than others. When valuing a reinsurance business, I like to consider what “average” results will be. In years like 2023/2024, results are better than average and should not be extrapolated, and this year will likely be worse. An investor should not assume $160 million of Q1 CA losses per year.

Cat losses are rising, but so should premiums

That said, every year there will be some cat losses, and there is clearly a trend of rising losses. Coastal areas have become more heavily populated, meaning the same storms create more losses, and weather patterns are likely contributing a role. As such, I would not totally ignore events like the CA losses, and I would expect the dollar value of losses to continue to rise in real terms.

Now, because of this risk, I expect demand for reinsurance to continue to rise, as insurers seek to offset some risk. Insofar as reinsurers can raise prices to offset a rising loss trend, higher losses may not be profit-destructive. We would just see higher premiums and higher claims, broadly offsetting.

Strong demand for reinsurance has brought significant capital into the sector from a decade ago. However, it has slowed, which I view positively for FIHL. Over the past three years, capital supporting reinsurance has only risen by 2% per year, slower than average inflation over this time, meaning that “real” capital has actually declined. This gives reinsurances more pricing power than at any point since 2018-2019. Increased demand for reinsurance combined with finite capital to provide insurance means that FIHL should be able to recoup losses and maintain profitable margins.

Core insurance is performing well

Now turning to its insurance business, FIHL wrote $512 million of insurance policies. It earned $82 million, down from $114 million, as its combined ratio rose by 10% to 84%. It continues to grow its financial product lines, particularly asset-backed finance.

Even though its combined ratio went up, its current year non-catastrophe losses were actually 7% better. Cat losses rose to 33% from 23%, and prior year favorable developments were smaller given some incremental losses tied to the Ukraine war, as it settled aviation claims. Essentially, the go-forward business is performing better, but we have some lingering impact from 2022 and earlier policies, which as I spoke about earlier, could lead to some more losses.

Importantly, we see insurers with solid pricing power. Premiums are rising 4% a year, which is solidly above the rate of inflation. Pricing in excess of inflation should support wider margins over the medium term, supporting an improved combined ratio. Now, because policies are typically written over 6–12 months if tariffs cause incremental inflation, this can temporarily weigh on margins, but as we saw post-COVID, insurers can recapture this later with price increases.

Its balance sheet and investment portfolio are strong

Fidelis also maintains a strong balance sheet, which enables it to ride through volatile claims. It has just $450 million of debt, for less than 17% debt to capital, below my 25% threshold of a solid balance sheet. As such, we have seen FIHL continue capital returns. It did $22 million of Q1 buybacks and another $20 million in April at $15.95. Buybacks have reduced its share count by about 6% from last year. $103 million remains on its authorization, and shares have a 2.4% dividend yield, which I view as secure.

Fidelis maintains a conservative investment portfolio. It has $4 billion of fixed income and cash and $200 million of other investments. As you can see, not only is its portfolio nearly entirely investment grade, but more than 80% is A-rated or higher, meaning there should be minimal if any credit losses, even in a moderate downturn. The portfolio has a ~5% book yield, and with a 2.9-year duration, this yield should be broadly stable over the next 12–18 months at least. Thanks to a favorable reinvestment environment, it generated $50 million of investment income in Q1 from $41 million a year ago.

Shares have upside past $22, as Russia-related losses pass

Elevated losses from Russia’s war have weighed on valuation, and shares trade at a very low multiple relative to peers. Shares are also trading at 0.76x book value of $21.45 vs. an industry average of 1.2x. A valuation discount is merited if FIHL continues to have disappointing underwriting results, but if the Russia conflict is truly a one-time event, this discount would not be merited, at least to this level.

At an 88% combined ratio with ~$2.4 billion of premiums and a 4.25% investment yield, FIHL has at least $2.50 in earnings power. Given the volatility of reinsurance results, I do expect FIHL to trade below less cat-exposed insurers. At 8x reinsurance earnings and 10x the rest of the business, fair value would still be over $22.50, or just above book value.

In general, an insurer with a profitable underwriting business should trade above book value in my view, as it has $3.1 billion of reserves, listed as liabilities. However, it will make money on its insurance policies, assuming an 87-90% combined ratio, and it does not pay claims until the future, giving it a lower present value. This is why insurers have a “float” against which they earn interest in buying fixed income. Haircutting reserves by ~10% to reflect this would unlock $250 million of value after taxes or about $2.20 per share.

This would imply $23.65 of economic book value. However, earlier I noted that I expected about a $1 impact from further losses on Russian planes that it will have to book this year. That would leave a pro forma book value of $22.65, which matches up with my P/E-based fair value.

This points to about 35% upside for shares, and with shares below book value, its buyback program is extremely accretive. While there may be some more bad news from its English court case, this appears to be more than fully priced in, and shares are trading as though war-linked losses will continue frequently, which is unlikely in my view. As we move past this event, underwriting results should improve, even with a steady catastrophe frequency. Additionally, after it cleans up its last Russian plane exposure, at such a discounted valuation with a highly specialized business, FIHL could be an attractive tuck-in acquisition target, though this is not my base case.

While the upcoming hurricane season and court case provide headline risk, this is more than factored into the share price. As such, I view FIHL as very attractive with an asymmetric risk/reward, and I view shares as a “strong buy” given the magnitude of potential upside.


Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. data.title: Nu Holdings: It Could Be An Ideal Time To Buy And Hold After Mixed Q1 Results
data.description:
Headquartered in São Paulo, Brazil, Nu Holdings (NYSE:NU), hereafter referred to as Nubank, given that it’s best known for its digital banking services, is the largest fintech bank in Latin America. Boasting around 118.6 million customers, predominantly in Brazil, Nubank has also built a sizable customer base throughout Mexico and Colombia. Having been floated as an initial public offering in December 2021, Nu Holdings, Nubank’s parent company, has grown from a valuation of $45 billion to a market capitalization of $65 billion at the time of writing.

Although the stock has faced a relatively challenging period following a mixed Q1 2025 earnings season, much of its financial shortfall can be attributed to its ambitious growth plans throughout LATAM and the rollout of new fintech tools to its user base. With a prospective 667 million population to reach throughout the region, Nubank certainly has plenty of room to rally if it can solidify its position as a neobank market leader.

Mixed Earnings

One of the headlines from Nubank’s first-quarter 2025 earnings was its reported 12 cents per share in earnings, missing estimates of 13 cents. Revenues of $3.2 billion also ticked slightly under the consensus estimate of $3.23 billion expected by analysts.

The misses had caused a brief period of volatility for the stock, but investor confidence in Nu’s underlying customer metrics has helped to deliver an element of resilience. Notably, the company added 4.3 million customers in Q1 2025, expanding its total customer base to 118.6 million throughout Brazil, Mexico, and Colombia. The monthly average revenue per active customer also increased 17% year-over-year to $11.20, suggesting that the rollout of new fintech services is resonating with its user base.

It’s also worth noting that Nu’s revenue miss is still significantly higher than the $2.74 billion that was collected over the same period of 2024. While net income under generally accepted accounting principles [GAAP] showed a rise to over $557 million from the year-ago profit of just under $379 million.

Focus on Expansion

Nubank doesn’t appear to be sitting still, despite being Latin America’s largest neobank. The fast-growing fintech has focused its attention on expanding beyond Brazil and into new nations within the region. The firm’s digital-only model has helped to introduce lower-cost banking to populations, helping to provide a unique selling proposition to users while providing competitive interest rates on credit cards and lending. With around 58% of Brazil’s adult population already customers, a prospective successful expansion to reach similar volumes of users throughout the LATAM region would see the firm attract 387 million customers.

Nubank is already working on an expansion into major markets like Mexico and Colombia and has doubled its customers in Mexico to 10 million while tripling to 2.5 million users in Colombia over the past year. With the recent regulatory approval from the Mexican National Banking and Securities Commission to transition into a full-service bank, it’s clear that investors can expect further market growth throughout Mexico in the coming months.

New Verticals

While Nubank’s growth in recent years throughout the LATAM region has been impressive, it’s been largely driven by credit growth, which carries an element of risk. It’s for this reason that clear evidence of an expansion beyond its traditional financial services into new verticals is another sign of future-proofing that can significantly benefit the stock and its investors in the long run.

The introduction of NuPay helps to implement digital payments for both online shopping and in-store transactions for users, allowing the challenger bank to replicate some of PayPal’s innovative components for its LATAM market. Additionally, Nu Travel is a tool that allows users to plan their vacations and book flights, hotels, and other travel-themed services within its app.

Nubank has also introduced Nu Marketplace, offering an intuitive shopping platform designed to fulfill a range of customer needs. Finally, customers can use NuCel, which is a dedicated mobile phone service, as another revenue stream for the company.

These verticals help to provide a level of resilience for Nubank beyond its credit-based revenue streams. With higher inflation causing interest rates in Brazil to rise to historical highs and becoming off-putting for prospective borrowers, opening the door to new markets could be an excellent avenue for sustainability for the stock.

Reaching the Unbanked

Perhaps most crucially, Nubank has the potential to become a market leader in reaching Latin America’s sizable unbanked and under-banked populations.

With an estimated 35% of adults in the LATAM region having no access to a bank account, while 42% are without a credit card, according to 2023 data from the Boston Consulting Group, Nubank may become a sole provider of invaluable financial services to millions.

This means that while Nubank faces a challenge to capture the $520 billion in assets held by Itaú Unibanco, the neobank has an advantage in reaching unbanked and under-banked populations throughout the region.

By offering checking and savings accounts, credit cards, investment and insurance products, and loans to the under-banked citizens throughout LATAM, Nubank currently has a market opportunity that’s largely untapped and uncontested.

Valuation

If Nubank can replicate its reach across 58% of Brazil’s adult population to the entirety of Latin America’s 667 million residents, it would represent a customer growth rate of 560%. Should this scaling up of operations be reflected in the firm’s stock, its market capitalization would reach $353 billion. This figure should be kept in mind as the full extent of the market opportunity facing Nubank, but the uneven regulatory landscape in the region will make it more difficult for the neobank to fulfill its ambitious expansion efforts in the same manner as at home in Brazil. However, the stock’s 20% growth in 2024 shows that there’s plenty of room to run despite a more challenging macroeconomic outlook this year, and there’s no reason why Nubank’s strong earnings expectations can’t push the stock towards an all-time high valuation of $80 billion by the end of 2025.

According to JPMorgan analyst Yuri Fernandes, Nubank is expected to grow its earnings by more than 30% over the next three years, a rate that’s difficult to find elsewhere, even in the burgeoning fintech landscape. Nubank, like many other fintech stocks, has endured a choppy first quarter of 2025 due to geopolitical and macroeconomic pressures as well as the company’s own expansion outlay. However, JPMorgan’s estimates for Nubank’s income for the years 2025-2026 to fall between $2.4 billion and $3.2 billion are indicative of healthy growth. Likewise, Bloomberg’s consensus of $2.6 billion to $3.6 billion over the same period shows that there’s plenty of room to run for the stock.

Given the neobank’s 48.7% revenue growth over the past 12 months, it’s clear that Nubank is well-positioned to scale its operations. But the question is whether the expectations for growth are priced into the stock’s current $63 billion valuation.

With around 60 million customers, Itaú Unibanco (ITUB) is Brazil’s largest traditional bank, and its market capitalization of $69 billion places it in similar territory to Nubank, despite the neobank possessing more customers domestically. Posting a Q1 2025 earnings of 9.4% year-over-year revenue increase to R$46.8 billion ($8.29 billion), Itaú Unibanco’s rate of growth is understandably far slower than its neobank counterpart’s 40% year-over-year to $3.2 billion, but still more than doubles that of Nubank at this stage in its development. Itaú Unibanco’s ROE of 22.5% on a consolidated basis and 23.7% in Brazil is dwarfed by Nubank’s annualized ROE of 27% for the first quarter of 2025. The traditional bank’s price-to-book (P/B) ratio is currently 1.46, while Nubank posted an eye-wateringly high 7.33 P/B, suggesting that the market has a significantly high expectation for Nubank’s future growth. This adds an element of risk that its stock value could fall significantly on any expansion setbacks in the future.

Risks

Given its speculative industry and growth strategy, there are some risks that Nubank must overcome to secure growth:

  • Credit risk: Because Nubank is focused on serving the unbanked and under-banked, the fintech is open to far greater credit risk than its counterparts in more established markets.

  • Regulatory uncertainty: Nubank may have recently gained regulatory approval in Mexico, but any barriers to entry for other markets could be hazardous for a stock that’s priced in for further growth.

  • Emerging competitors: The fintech landscape is constantly evolving, and new challengers can emerge to eat into Nubank’s market share with little warning.

Despite these risks, Nubank remains an exceptional fintech within the LATAM region, and there are few signs of the firm slowing its growth strategy into valuable new markets.

Conclusion

Nubank is a rarity in the 21st Century as a US-listed company that specializes in delivering valuable banking services to new markets. Should digital financial services resonate with users throughout Latin America as they did with Brazil, in which Nubank serves 58% of the population, then reaching 58% of LATAM’s total population would represent a prospective 387 million-strong customer base.

Nubank’s commitment to branching into the facilitation of digital payments, supporting holiday bookings, and providing a cellular network shows that the firm is intent on expanding across many different verticals. Should Nubank successfully grow into uncontested dominance throughout LATAM, it has the potential to surpass the prevalence of PayPal. Should we see the level of bullish investor sentiment sweep into fintech stocks in the same way as in 2020 and 2021, then Nubank is a stock that can gain some serious traction.

data.title: Pagaya: A Deep Dive Into Its Untapped Value Proposition
data.description:
Since last year, I have been bullish on Pagaya Technologies Ltd. (NASDAQ:PGY), rating it as a buy-in May last year and last February following the release of its FY 2024 earnings report. The core of my bullish thesis on Pagaya is its growing network volume, increasing funding partners, improving profitability metrics, and efforts to reduce its risk exposure.

Earlier this month, Pagaya reported its Q1 2025 earnings which marked a new chapter in its story as the company posted its first GAAP profitable quarter as a public company. The company’s impressive results were driven by operating leverage and better monetization of its partners which is leading to expanding margins. At the same time, Pagaya’s risk retention continues to improve as the performance of its loans is driving forward flow agreements which helps reduce its reliance on ABS issuances that require a higher risk retention rate.

With that in mind, Pagaya’s personal loan ABS issued prior to September 2024 has recently received a rating upgrade by KBRA, validating its AI underwriting. In my opinion, this upgrade could help Pagaya secure additional forward flow agreements in the short-term, and in turn, further reduce its risk exposure. In light of these factors, I’m upgrading Pagaya to a strong buy with a price target of $51, implying a 228% upside from current levels.

Q1 Overview

In Q1 2025, Pagaya reported 18% YoY growth in total revenues from $245.3 million to $290 million, driven by fee revenues increasing 19% from $237 million to $282.7 million. The company’s YoY fee revenue growth occurred despite network volume slightly declining by 0.8% YoY from $2.42 billion to $2 billion, meaning that its take rate expanded YoY from 9.8% to 11.8%. In my opinion, the company’s expanding take rate highlights the strength of its offering as its partners are willing to pay more to underwrite loans through its platform.

As such, Pagaya’s fee revenue less production costs (FRLPC) increased 25.5% YoY from $92.1 million to $115.6 million. As a percentage of network volume, FRLPC reached a record 4.8% in Q1 2025 compared to 3.8% in the prior year.

Pagaya’s expanding FRLPC margin is a direct result of its strategy of focusing on profitable growth. To that end, the company reduced its exposure to the SFR (Single-Family Rental) loan vertical which has a lower FRLPC profile, leading to a slight decline in its network volume. Excluding that impact, the company’s network volume grew 26% YoY and 6% sequentially. At the same time, FRLPC as a percentage of network volume reached 5.2% in Q1, excluding the impact of the SFR vertical.

In addition to its expanding margins, Pagaya’s Q1 results showed significant cost efficiencies as its operating costs only increased 2% YoY from $237.6 million to $242.3 million. In this way, the company reported a substantial 520% YoY growth in operating income from $7.7 million at an operating margin of 3.1% to $47.7 million at an operating margin of 16.4%.

Similarly, the company’s adjusted EBITDA increased 100% YoY from $39.8 million at a margin of 16.2% to a record $79.6 million at a margin of 27.4%. With impressive topline growth and operating leverage, Pagaya reported its first GAAP profitable quarter as a public company as it reported a GAAP net income of $7.9 million compared to a GAAP loss of $21.2 million in the prior year.

In terms of cash flow, Pagaya reported 144% YoY growth in free cash flow from $12.6 million at an FCF margin of 5.1% to $30.6 million at a margin of 10.6%.

The company’s improving free cash flow generation provides it with greater flexibility to further develop its AI models to improve credit assessment and explore expansion opportunities into new verticals. At the same time, the significant expansion in FCF margin indicates that Pagaya’s business model is maturing and proving its viability, which could reflect on its valuation as its business could demand a higher valuation multiple.

Moving on to its balance sheet, Pagaya exited Q1 with $229.6 million in cash and held $760 million of investments in loans and securities. The company also has a total debt balance of $672.2 million, of which $536.4 million are long-term debt.

Looking forward, Pagaya is guiding Q2 network volume between $2.3 to $2.5 billion, total revenue between $290 to $310 million, adjusted EBITDA between $75 to $90 million, and GAAP income between breakeven to $10 million. For the full year, the company expects network volume between $9.5 to $11 billion, a reduction from its original guidance issued in Q4 2024. However, the company raised its revenue guidance to a range between $1.175 to $1.3 billion, adjusted EBITDA between $290 to $330 million, and GAAP income between $10 to $45 million.

Better Loan Performance Will Drive Lower Risk Retention

As mentioned earlier, Pagaya exited Q1 with $760 million of investments in loans and securities. The majority of these loans are 2025 and 2024 vintages, representing 70% of the total loans on its balance sheet, while 2023 and 2022, prior vintages represent 19% and 11% of its total loans, respectively.

The significance of this is that the newer loan vintages are performing much better than older vintages, as witnessed by the substantial $235 million impairment charge on poor-performing loans spanning 2021 through 2023. This can be seen by the stable delinquency rates for Pagaya’s personal loan portfolio and declining delinquencies for its auto loan portfolio.

These results are the main driver behind Pagaya’s improving risk retention rates. In Q1, the company purchased loans worth $81.9 million, compared to $261.6 million in the prior year and $155.2 million in the prior quarter. This means that the company retained only 3.41% of its network volume during the quarter, compared to 10.82% and 5.96% in the prior year and quarter, respectively.

The improving performance of Pagaya’s loans is being reflected in its ability to increasingly secure future funding. This is evident by the forward flow agreement with Blue Owl for $2.4 billion over 24 months and the $800 million it raised through ABS in April for its personal loan and auto loan verticals.

Moreover, the company recently received a rating upgrade to its personal loan ABS issued prior to September 2024 by KBRA. This rating upgrade was mainly due to KBRA’s analysis indicating that these notes are exhibiting credit improvements as they have received timely interest payments.

This rating upgrade is a significant validation of the strength and accuracy of Pagaya’s AI underwriting, even in potentially challenging economic environments. In my opinion, this could further improve investor confidence in the company’s loans which could be reflected in lower funding costs, attract new capital to Pagaya’s network, and potentially help it secure additional forward flow agreements in the near future. Accordingly, I expect Pagaya’s risk retention rate to continue declining to around 3% by the end of 2025.

POS Inflection

In addition to potentially helping it secure additional forward flow agreements, the improving performance of Pagaya’s loans is a tailwind to its expansion in the POS vertical, in my opinion. The company recently announced its inaugural AAA-rated $300 million ABS program that is focused on POS financing. This program has a revolving nature over 18 months, allowing the company to reinvest proceeds as interest payments are made, which provides Pagaya with the capacity to fund more than $1 billion of POS loans.

In my opinion, the launch of this ABS program is a major inflection point for Pagaya’s POS vertical, which is already its fastest-growing vertical, per management in the Q1 earnings call. As is, this ABS program allows the company to further penetrate the POS market by enabling its POS lending partners, namely Klarna, to approve a larger number of customers at the point of sale. This is especially significant since it could help Pagaya’s take rate expand over time if these loans are performing well.

With that in mind, the new POS ABS program is targeting borrowers with FICO scores higher than 600, which carries some credit risk. Therefore, it is critical that these loans perform well for Pagaya to expand its take rate and improve its bottom-line by reducing its funding costs. As is, Finsight states that Pagaya’s bonds will yield 1.75% more than benchmark treasury bonds, whereas Affirm (AFRM), which targets borrowers with higher credit scores, had its latest bond yield 0.95% higher than treasuries.

Valuation

At the midpoint of its full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance and its current share price of $15.72, Pagaya is trading at a 5.27 EV/adjusted EBITDA multiple. This indicates that Pagaya remains extremely undervalued relative to the sector median of 14, despite its improving profitability. By applying the sector median EV/EBITDA, my price target for Pagaya is $51.50 per share, representing a 228% upside from its current valuation.

Risks

Although I remain bullish on Pagaya, there are some risks to consider. The main risk to my thesis is the company’s sensitivity to economic downturns. While Pagaya’s AI models are showing significant improvements in underwriting, higher-than-expected job losses or consumer deleveraging efforts could lead to a spike in delinquencies across all loan portfolios, including newer vintages.

Another risk to consider is Pagaya’s reliance on the willingness of institutional investors to buy its ABS or enter forward flow agreements. If a significant shift in investor sentiment away from consumer credit occurs, Pagaya’s funding channels could dry up and potentially see it fund a higher number of loans from its balance sheet, increasing its exposure to credit risk.

Moreover, the company’s latest POS ABS could present a challenge to credit risk due to its focus on riskier consumers. Although Pagaya’s AI is designed to minimize risk, any significant underperformance in these loans could put pressure on the program’s perceived quality. This could affect future ABS pricing, which would be a headwind to profitability.

Conclusion

In summary, I remain bullish on Pagaya and maintain my belief that its shares are extremely undervalued. The company has turned a corner in Q1 2025 with its first GAAP profitable quarter as a public company thanks to its robust topline growth, expanding margin, and efficiently managing costs. This improved financial performance is aided by the improvements in its loans, which are driving increased trust from its funding partners through forward flow agreements.

In my opinion, Pagaya is likely to secure additional forward flow agreements in the near term thanks to its loans’ improving performance, which was validated by a recent rating upgrade by KBRA. Moreover, Pagaya’s expansion in the rapidly growing POS vertical with the launch of its inaugural POS ABS program could further help its future growth by potentially expanding its take rate over time, if these loans replicate the performance of its personal and auto loans. It is for these reasons that I’m upgrading Pagaya to a strong buy with a price target of $51, implying a 228% upside from its current valuation.
